frameworks.name: signaling theory
frameworks.one_line: self-handicapping as a costly signal of competence to observers

frameworks.name: formal optimal-choice model
frameworks.one_line: chooses degree of self-handicapping balancing performance and signaling effects

frameworks.name: naive vs sophisticated observer inference
frameworks.one_line: observer type modulates how handicap is interpreted

core_question: Why, when, and how do people self-handicap to manage perceived competence?
key_assumptions: agents choose handicap degree to maximize a competence-signaling payoff
observers update beliefs about competence given visible handicap
naive and sophisticated observers infer differently from the same handicap
manipulations: likelihood that handicapping affects task outcome
outcome valence (success vs failure) following handicap
key_findings: self-handicapping is more frequent when unlikely to affect the outcome
self-handicapping raises perceived competence in naive observers
with sophisticated observers, handicapping is less effective after failure
interpretations: self-handicapping is a strategic competence signal whose value depends on observer inference
the formal theory also accounts for several prior self-handicapping findings
